coredata.dc:title: Room-temperature synthesis of BiOI/Graphene oxide foam composite for phenol removal under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: He, Rongan
Lou, Zhimei
Gui, Jing
Tang, Bo
Xu, Difa
coredata.dc:description: The coupling of graphene-based materials is a widely adopted method to effectively separate photo-induced electrons and holes, and consequently, improve the performance of photocatalysts. However, the surface modification of semiconductors with graphene materials can block incident light; this is undesirable for the activity enhancement of photocatalysts. To solve this problem, a composite of graphene oxide foam coated with bismuth oxyiodide (GOF–BiOI) was synthesized at room temperature using an in-situ deposition approach. In the composite, BiOI flake arrays stand vertically and uniformly on the surface of GOF. Furthermore, the as-prepared GOF–BiOI exhibited a higher photocatalytic activity than BiOI and GO-modified BiOI for the oxidization of phenol under visible light. Based on a collective analysis of the reactive species, photoluminescence, light absorption, textural and morphological properties, the enhanced photocatalytic activity of GOF–BiOI was attributed to the electron trapping role of GOF, which effectively suppress charge recombination, and to the unique structure of GOF–BiOI, which favored not only light absorption but also the attachment of reactive materials and the surface of BiOI.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bismuth oxyiodide
Graphene oxide foam
In-situ synthesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Specific surface area and pore structure

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of BiOI, GOF, BiOI-GO and GOF–BiOI.
FESEM images of BiOI (a), GOF (b), GOF–BiOI (c) and magnified image of GOF–BiOI (d).
TEM image (a), HRTEM image (b) and EDS spectrum (c) of GOF–BiOI.
Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution (inset) of GOF (black), BiOI (red), BiOI–GO (purple), and GOF–BiOI (blue).
DRS spectra of BiOI (red), GOF (black), GOF–BiOI (blue), and BiOI–GO (purple).
Raman spectra of BiOI (red), BiOI–GO (purple), and GOF–BiOI (blue), under a laser excitation of 633 nm.
PL emission spectra of BiOI (red), BiOI–GO (purple), and GOF–BiOI (blue) under exciting light of 510 nm.

(a) photocatalytic performance of TiO2, BiOI, BiOI–GO, and GOF–BiOI for phenol removal, under visible light from an LED lamp (λ > 400 nm), and (b) temporal UV–vis spectra of phenol solution over GOF-BiOI.
Photocatalytic performance of BiOI, BiOI–GO, and GOF–BiOI in the presence of different scavengers, under visible light from an LED lamp (λ > 400 nm, t = 90 min).
I-t curves of BiOI (red), BiOI–GO (purple), and GOF–BiOI (blue), in 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution under visible light from an LED lamp (λ > 400 nm).
Illustration of the superiority of GOF in the performance improvement of GOF-BiOI.

head.ce:title: Room-temperature synthesis of BiOI/Graphene oxide foam composite for phenol removal under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Province Key Laboratory of Applied Environmental Photocatalysis, Changsha University, Changsha 410022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Province Key Laboratory of Applied Environmental Photocatalysis, Changsha University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hunan Province Key Laboratory of Applied Environmental Photocatalysis, Changsha University, Changsha 410022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The coupling of graphene-based materials is a widely adopted method to effectively separate photo-induced electrons and holes, and consequently, improve the performance of photocatalysts. However, the surface modification of semiconductors with graphene materials can block incident light; this is undesirable for the activity enhancement of photocatalysts. To solve this problem, a composite of graphene oxide foam coated with bismuth oxyiodide (GOF–BiOI) was synthesized at room temperature using an in-situ deposition approach. In the composite, BiOI flake arrays stand vertically and uniformly on the surface of GOF. Furthermore, the as-prepared GOF–BiOI exhibited a higher photocatalytic activity than BiOI and GO-modified BiOI for the oxidization of phenol under visible light. Based on a collective analysis of the reactive species, photoluminescence, light absorption, textural and morphological properties, the enhanced photocatalytic activity of GOF–BiOI was attributed to the electron trapping role of GOF, which effectively suppress charge recombination, and to the unique structure of GOF–BiOI, which favored not only light absorption but also the attachment of reactive materials and the surface of BiOI.

ce:section.ce:para: A negative aspect of economic development is that environment pollution has been posing an increasing threat to ecosystems and human health in the last few decades. Hence, eco-friendly photocatalysis-based environmental decontamination has attracted increased attention and is considered a promising pathway for practical wastewater treatment. In particular, visible-light-responsive photocatalysts are recommended, because they can more efficiently utilize solar energy for effective pollutant removal [1].

There are many semiconductor photocatalysts that exhibit a narrow band gap and good visible-light response, such as Bi2WO6 [2], g-C3N4 [3], Bi2O3 [4], CeO2 [5], Ag2CrO4 [6], Fe2O3 [7,8], BiOI [9,10], Bi4O5I2 [11], CdS [12], CdSe [13], and MoO3 [14]. However, the photocatalytic performances of these photocatalysts are not ideal when they are used alone. This is mainly due to the rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, which leads to their consumption [15–20]. Although a large number of electrons and holes are generated on these photocatalysts under visible light excitation, only a few of them can actually reach the photocatalyst surface and participate in the reaction. To increase the amount of carriers available on the surface of photocatalysts, electrons and holes should be separated effectively. Coupling these photocatalysts with conductive noble metals or carbonaceous materials can effectively capture the photogenerated electrons from semiconductors and thus facilitate the separation of the carriers [21]. Therefore, many noble metals and carbonaceous materials have been adopted as the electrons trapping agents, including Pt [22], Pd [10], carbon nanotubes [23], such as carbon quantum dots [24,25], graphene oxide (GO) [26–28] and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [29–31].

Among these conductive materials, graphene-based compounds exhibit excellent conductivity and can act as electron-trapping agents [32–36]. Hence, they have been widely applied to enhance the activity of photocatalysts [37–46]. Furthermore, the main component of graphene materials is the earth-abundant element, carbon. Therefore, graphene-based materials are more widely available than noble metals, and are superior to them in terms of applicability. However, in most graphene/semiconductor composites, the applied GO or rGO covers the surface of the semiconductors [37,39,47–50]. Owing to this blockage by graphene sheets, the light absorption of the photocatalysts is affected, and the contact between the photocatalysts and the reactants is also inhibited. Consequently, the improvement in the performance of the composites is limited. Typically, there is an optimal content of graphene materials. When the content of graphene materials exceeds this optimal value, the activities of graphene-modified photocatalysts decrease [51–56]. To overcome this, it is better to locate nanoscale photocatalysts on the exterior of the graphene-based materials. In such an infrastructure, the graphene will not prevent the photocatalysts from absorbing light and contacting with the reactants. Thus, these graphene-based materials will facilitate the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, without inhibiting the performance of the photocatalysts. Unfortunately, graphene materials are soft and do not meet the requirements of supports for photocatalysts. Furthermore, graphene materials are expensive and are not suitable for use in large quantities. Therefore, the construction of self-standing hollow porous networks of graphene or graphene oxide will be a desirable solution.

Recently, a series of foam-like graphene-based materials have attracted increased attention for application in supercapacitors, electromagnetic wave absorption materials, sensor and solar energy cells [57–61]. These graphene-based foams possess 3D cellular architectures with interconnected open pores [62]. This kind of cellular architecture can endow graphene-based foams with excellent mechanical properties and lightweight, enabling them to be used as self-standing supports [63]. Furthermore, owing to the predominantly open porous networks, graphene-based foams usually possess high specific surface area [62,64,65]. For these reasons, graphene-based foam is an evidently suitable candidate as a substrate of photocatalysts. Among graphene-based foams, graphene oxide foam (GOF) is a more favorable substrate because of its better wettability. However, the role of GOF will be restricted unless the photocatalyst can be effectively deposited on its surface. This is because GOF cannot capture the photogenerated electrons from a photocatalyst if they are not in contact with each other.

To effectively use GOF and promote the performance of a photocatalyst, it is also necessary to selectively deposit the photocatalyst on the foam via an appropriate method. Although the depositions of TiO2 [66], WO3 [67] and ZnO [68–70] on graphene foam have been reported, it is still a challenging work with respect to photocatalysts. When as-prepared graphene foam is immersed in a reaction solution that is commonly used to prepare a pure photocatalyst, some of the photocatalyst particles may precipitate directly in the solution, rather than deposit on the graphene foam. Only when the photocatalyst preferentially nucleates on the surface of the graphene foam can it be selectively deposited on the foam.

Hence, we developed a facile room-temperature hydrolysis method for in-situ deposition of bismuth oxyiodide (BiOI) nanoplates onto GOF. The prepared GOF–BiOI composite exhibited a higher photocatalytic activity than BiOI and GO-modified BiOI.

All the reagents, including Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (Guangdong Chemical Reagent Engineering Technological Research and Development Center), KI (Guangdong Guanghua Chemical Factory Co. Ltd.), ethylene glycol (EG), ethanol, and phenol (Tianjin Guangcheng Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd.) were analytically pure and used without further treatment. A 1.0 mg/mL graphene oxide (GO) suspension was prepared from natural graphite powder (> 99.8%, Alfa Aesar) via a modified Hummers′ method.

Hierarchical BiOI particles were synthesized using a hydrolysis method. Briefly, 1.5 g Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 0.50 g KI were dissolved in 50 mL EG; then, 20 mL distilled water was added dropwise into the EG solution while stirring. The mixed solution was stirred at room temperature for 2 h until a red powder formed. Thereafter, the red powder was collected by centrifugation, washed several times with distilled water and ethanol, and it was dried at 80 °C overnight.

To prepare GO-decorated BiOI, we used a self-assembly method that has been used before [28]. In short, a well-dispersed 0.1 mg/mL GO suspension was firstly prepared by diluting 2 mL of the original GO suspension with 18 mL ethanol, under magnetic stirring and ultrasonic treatment. Then, 0.2 g of as-prepared BiOI microspheres was dispersed in the GO suspension while stirring for 2 h. After centrifugal separation, washing, and drying, a dark-orange precipitate was obtained, and the sample was labeled as BiOI–GO.

GOF was prepared using a reported immerging and annealing method [57]. Firstly, a commercial polyurethane (PU) sponge block was dipped entirely into a GO suspension. Then, the GO-soaked PU sponge was burned in an ethanol flame to eliminate the PU sponge. Thus, GOF was obtained.

GO-foam-supported BiOI composite (GOF–BiOI) was fabricated via an in-situ synthesis at room temperature, by depositing BiOI flakes on the GOF. Briefly, 1.5 g Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 0.50 g KI were dissolved in 50 mL EG; then, 20 mL distilled water was added dropwise into the EG solution while stirring. The GO foam was immerged in the mixed solution at ambient temperature for 2 h until a red powder appeared on the surface. Thereafter, the composite was collected by sucking out the solution, and poached several times with distilled water and ethanol. After drying at 80 °C overnight, the final composite was obtained, and labeled as GOF–BiOI.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Bruker (Germany) D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer. The morphologies were surveyed using a JEOL (Japan) JSM-7500F field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) instrument and a FEL (USA) Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (TEM), equipped with electronic data systems (EDS) attachments. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of the samples were recorded using a Quantachrome (USA) Autosorb-iQ gas adsorption analyzer. Diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) measurements were carried out on a Shimadzu (Japan) UV-2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired using a Hitachi (Japan) F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Raman spectra were measured on a Renishaw (UK) InVia Raman spectrometer using excitation by a laser at 633 nm. Photoelectrochemical tests of the samples were conducted on a CHI660C electrochemical analyzer, in a standard three-electrode cell. An Ag/AgCl electrode and Pt wire were the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. Fluorine-doped tin oxide conductive glass (FTO) coated with the sample was the working electrode.

The photocatalytic performance of the samples was evaluated according to the efficiency of phenol removal. Briefly, 15 mL of 100 mg/L phenol solution was decanted into a Petri dish (60 mm in diameter); then, 50 mg of the photocatalyst was added. Thereafter, the Petri dish was covered with a glass cap and irradiated under an LED spotlight lamp (60 W, λ > 400 nm). The change in the concentration of phenol was estimated based on the temporal UV–vis spectra of the phenol solutions, recorded on a Shimadzu (Japan) UVmini-1240 spectrophotometer. Beside as-prepared samples, the performance of commercial TiO2 (P25) was also tested for comparison.

Fig. 1 compares the XRD patterns of pure BiOI, BiOI–GO, GOF, and GOF–BiOI. The pattern of GOF shows only one broad weak peak at 21.7°, indicating the low crystallinity of GO [53–57]. For BiOI, BiOI–GO, and GOF–BiOI, all the evident diffraction peaks match the tetragonal phase of BiOI (JCPDS PDF-10-0445). The peaks of GOF–BiOI at 29.7°, 31.7°, 45.5°, and 55.2° are weaker and broader than those of pure BiOI, because the BiOI slices grown on the GOF are much smaller than pure BiOI. The peaks of BiOI–GO are also broader than those of BiOI; however, the main reason for this is the shield effect of GO covering the surface. The broad diffraction peak of GO could not be identified in the pattern of BiOI–GO, because of the low content of GO (1 wt%). The GO content of GOF–BiOI was higher than that of BiOI–GO. However, the broad peak of GO was still unrecognizable in the pattern. This was because the GOF was mostly coated with BiOI nanoplates, and its diffraction was shielded.

The images of the as-prepared GOF, BiOI, and GOF–BiOI composite are presented in Fig. 2 . Pure BiOI generally comprised nanoparticles assembled from nanoplates. During the formation of BiOI, the initially formed BiOI nanolamellas prefer to grow together to reduce the surface energy, and then continue to grow to form hierarchical structures. The as-prepared GOF exhibited a frame-like structure with interconnected macropores. Evidently, the falseworks composed of GO nanosheets possess enough mechanical strength and can make the whole structure self-standing. When the surface of the GOF is filled with BiOI nanoparticles, its integral framework remains unchanged, as shown in Fig. 2c. More interestingly, in the magnified image (Fig. 2d), all the BiOI nanoplates stand vertically and trimly on the surface of the GOF, unlike the large nanoplate-assembled microspheres of BiOI.

The unique morphology of the BiOI flakes on the GOF can be attributed to an in-situ nucleation and growth of BiOI nanoplates. In the preparation of GOF–BiOI, Bi3+ ions were first deposited on the surface of the GOF under electrostatic attraction, and they then grew into nanoplates in situ. To achieve the in-situ growth of BiOI, the concentration of Bi3+, I−, and EG should be properly adjusted. In such a mixed solution, the saturation of BiOI can only meet the requirements of deposition on the GOF but not those for nucleation in solution. Hence, BiOI does not precipitate directly in the solution. Furthermore, owing to the immobilization role of the GOF, the formed BiOI slices grow separately, instead of growing together. Consequently, uniform, small BiOI plates are formed on the GOF.

Fig. 3 a and b show the TEM and HRTEM images of GOF–BiOI, respectively. In Fig. 3a, BiOI flakes dispersed uniformly on the GOF. The clear fringes in Fig. 3b corresponds to the (1 1 0) facet of BiOI. No BiOI flakes can be found outside the GO sheets, indicating that BiOI flakes was deposited on the GO sheets, instead physically mixed with GO. The existence of Bi, O, I was confirmed by EDS spectrum of GOF-BiOI in Fig. 3c. The content of Bi and C was evaluated to be 12 wt% and 72 wt%, respectively. Considering the equal ratio of Bi, O, I in tetragonal phase BiOI, the content of BiOI was estimated to be 22 wt%. Correspondingly, the content of GOF was estimated to be 78 wt%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specific surface area and pore structure
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 illustrates the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of BiOI, BiOI–GO, GOF–BiOI, and GOF. Based on the adsorption isotherms, the specific surface areas of BiOI, BiOI–GO, GOF–BiOI, and GOF were calculated to be 34, 46, 63, and 82 m2/g, respectively. In the isotherms of GOF, a large hysteresis loop exists almost throughout the pressure range, indicating a large pore volume. This is in agreement with the calculated pore size distribution and the morphology of GOF. The high specific surface area of GOF is mainly attributed to its open porous structure [65]. When GOF was deposited with BiOI flakes, the specific surface area of composite decreased a little, because of the higher specific weight of BiOI. However, the specific surface area of GOF–BiOI is still higher than BiOI and BiOI–GO. This is mainly attributed to the porous structure of GOF. The large hysteresis loop of GOF–BiOI resembles that of GOF, indicating that the pore structure of GOF is dominant in the composite. BiOI–GO and BiOI have similar pore structures. The specific surface area of BiOI–GO is higher than that of BiOI, because of the newly formed micropores between BiOI and GO in BiOI–GO.

The DRS spectra of GOF, BiOI, BiOI–GO, and the GOF–BiOI composite are shown in Fig. 5 . GOF can absorb the entire spectrum of light, owing to its black color. The absorption edges of BiOI, GOF–BiOI, and BiOI–GO are straight and steep, indicating the light absorption of BiOI via the intrinsic band-gap transition. The absorption edge of BiOI was estimated to be 640 nm, and the band gap (Eg) was estimated to be 1.93 eV. For light below 500 nm, the photo-absorption intensity of GOF, GOF–BiOI, and BiOI–GO are nearly the same, and slightly stronger than that of pure BiOI. In this range, the content of GO has little effect on the photo-absorption of the samples because of the strong absorption of BiOI. Over 640 nm, the photo-absorption of BiOI is weak; therefore, the influence of the GO content becomes evident. In GOF–BiOI, the content of GO is much higher than that in BiOI–GO; consequently, the light absorption of GOF–BiOI is stronger than that of BiOI–GO.

The Raman spectra of BiOI, BiOI–GO, and GOF–BiOI are given in Fig. 6 . Two characteristic peaks of the D band (1340 cm−1) and G band (1595 cm−1) of GO appeared in the spectra of both BiOI–GO and GOF–BiOI; these two peaks were not observed in the spectrogram of pure BiOI. This reveals the existence of GO in GOF–BiOI and BiOI–GO. The peak intensity of GOF–BiOI is lower than that of BiOI–GO, though GOF–BiOI has a higher GO content. This is the result of the location of GO. In BiOI–GO, GO is coated with BiOI, and the Raman signal of GO is much stronger. In GOF–BiOI, by contrast, GO was covered by BiOI. Therefore, the Raman signal of GO became relatively weaker.

The PL spectra of BiOI, GOF–BiOI, and BiOI–GO were recorded under the excitation of a 510 nm laser; they are shown in Fig. 7 . The PL peak at 769 nm was observed in all the three spectra. The PL intensity of pure BiOI is the strongest, whereas the PL emissions of GOF–BiOI and BiOI–GO are much weaker. This was due to the influences of GO on the generation and separation of carriers in BiOI. In BiOI–GO, the exterior GO sheets can simultaneously improve the separation of electrons and holes, and decrease the light absorption of BiOI. Both these actions reduce the PL emission of BiOI–GO. On the contrary, in GOF–BiOI, BiOI nanoplates are located on the exterior of the GOF, and the shield effect of GO is non-existent. Therefore, the decrease in the PL emission definitely indicates the facilitated separation of the photo-induced electrons and holes.

In the photocatalytic reaction, the concentration of phenol does not change during the light irradiation (Fig. 8 a) if no photocatalyst is added, reflecting the stability of phenol under visible light. This is because phenol is transparent and is not easily excited by visible light. The performance of the photocatalysts could be easily estimated from the phenol concentration changes under visible light irradiation. BiOI–GO exhibited an improved photocatalytic activity, compared with BiOI, due to the electron-trapping role of GO. Among these three photocatalysts, GOF–BiOI showed the best activity, and the characteristic peak of phenol (at 269 nm) decreased rapidly, indicating a quick removal of phenol (Fig. 8b). Considering that the BiOI content in GOF–BiOI (22 wt%) is much lower than that in BiOI–GO (99 wt%), it can be easily inferred that the higher activity of GOF–BiOI is ascribable to the higher utilization efficiency of light irradiation and photogenerated carriers. The unique structure of the BiOI-coated GOF played an important role.

To determine the reactive species, isopropanol (IPA), p-benzoquinone (BQ) and triethanolamine (TEOA) were applied to scavenge OH, O2 −, and holes (h+), respectively, in order to elucidate their roles during the photocatalytic reaction. The conversions of phenol in the presence of different trapping agents are compared in Fig. 9 (reaction time t = 90 min). The performance of the three samples was almost unaffected by IPA, indicating that OH was not the main reactive species. The photocatalytic activity of BiOI was significantly affected by TEOA, whereas BQ had less impact on its activity, suggesting that h+ was the main reactive species in the photocatalytic reaction over BiOI. In the case of BiOI–GO, h+ was the main reactive species, whereas O2 − played a moderate role. As for GOF–BiOI, the influence of h+ was greater than that of O2 − but not as significant as in the cases of BiOI and BiOI–GO. This reflects an integrated effect of the composite. When TEOA consumes h+ on the valance band (VB) of BiOI, more electrons can migrate to the GOF, on which more O2 − will be generated by the reaction between electrons and O2. Therefore, the influence of TEOA would be reduced. Although a similar phenomenon occurred on BiOI–GO, the influence of TEOA was not significantly reduced. This was due to the low content of GO in BiOI–GO. Hence, the improvement in the activity of BiOI–GO was also limited.

The I–t curves of the BiOI, BiOI-GO, and GOF–BiOI photo-electrodes are compared in Fig. 10 . When the LED light was turned on, the photocurrent increased sharply, and when the light was switched off, the photocurrent dropped steeply to zero. This was the result of the swift generation and migration of electrons and holes over the photocatalysts. The BiOI–GO electrode exhibited a higher photocurrent than the BiOI electrode, indicating that the introduction of GO facilitated the migration and separation of the photogenerated carriers. Because the BiOI in BiOI–GO is similar to pure BiOI, the output of electrons and holes on BiOI–GO cannot be higher than that in pure BiOI. Therefore, the higher photocurrent of BiOI–GO is attributable to the more efficient separation of the carriers. The photocurrent over the GOF–BiOI electrodes was much higher than that over BiOI–GO. One reason for this was the higher content of GO in GOF–BiOI. Because GO can improve the carrier separation, higher GO content can provide more channels for the migration of electrons. As a result, electrons transfer faster in GOF–BiOI, and the photocurrent increases accordingly. Moreover, a higher carrier production may also play an important role. As mentioned earlier, GOF–BiOI possesses a higher specific surface area, and BiOI nanoplates form on the surface of the GOF. Such a structure enables the BiOI nanoplates to absorb more radiation and generate more electrons and holes. It is possible for GOF–BiOI to produce more electrons and holes than pure BiOI, even though the BiOI content in GOF–BiOI is lower than that in pure BiOI. In summary, the high photocurrent achieved on GOF–BiOI is due to the combined roles of higher generation and effective separation of electrons and holes.

Based on the above analysis, the photocatalysis mechanism of GOF–BiOI is shown in Fig. 11 . The BiOI slices are uniformly dispersed on the surface of the GOF, and fully absorb incident light and generate carriers. The photogenerated holes can migrate quickly to the surface, and participate in the oxidation of phenol, because of the small size of the BiOI slices. The photoelectrons, on the other hand, are transferred to the GOF and react with O2 to form O2 −. As for BiOI–GO, because GO is wrapped on the surface of BiOI, the absorption of light, the excitation of carriers, and the contact with reactants will be affected.

A GOF–BiOI composite was fabricated using a facile in-situ deposition method at ambient temperature. During the preparation, BiOI nucleated and grew into nanoflakes on the surface of GOF, instead of forming microspheres in the solution without GOF. The as-prepared GOF–BiOI composite exhibited a band structure similar to that of pure BiOI. Owing to the unique structure of BiOI grown on the exterior of the GOF, the facilitated carrier separation, and the improved contact between BiOI and the reactants, the photocatalytic performance of the GOF–BiOI composite was greatly improved, exceeding that of pure BiOI and GO-modified BiOI.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that there is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (21871030), the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province of China (2018JJ2456, 2018JJ2457) and the Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department of China (18A370).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visible/solar light active photocatalysts for organic effluent treatment: Fundamentals, mechanisms and parametric review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.V. Bora, R.K. Mewada, Visible/solar light active photocatalysts for organic effluent treatment: Fundamentals, mechanisms and parametric review, Renew. Sust. Energ. Rev. 76 (2017) 1393-1421.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile fabrication of well-polarized Bi2WO6 nanosheets with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Zhang, J. Cao, G. Huang, J. Li, D. Li, W. Yao, T. Zeng, Facile fabrication of well-polarized Bi2WO6 nanosheets with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity, Catal. Sci. Technol. 8 (2018) 6420-6428.
J. Fu, Q. Xu, J. Low, C. Jiang, J. Yu, Ultrathin 2D/2D WO3/g-C3N4 step-scheme H2-production photocatalyst, Appl. Catal. B 243 (2019) 556-565.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphite oxide-assisted sonochemical preparation of α-Bi2O3 nanosheets and their high-efficiency visible light photocatalytic activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Xie, Q. Su, J. Zhang, G. Du, B. Xu, Graphite oxide-assisted sonochemical preparation of α-Bi2O3 nanosheets and their high-efficiency visible light photocatalytic activity, J. Mater. Sci. 49 (2014) 218-224.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis and characterization of CeO2 nanowires for using as a photocatalytic material

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Phuruangrat, S. Thongtem, T. Thongtem, Microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis and characterization of CeO2 nanowires for using as a photocatalytic material, Mater. Lett. 196 (2017) 61-63.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced photocatalytic activity and stability of Z-scheme Ag2CrO4-GO composite photocatalysts for organic pollutant degradation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Xu, B. Cheng, S. Cao, J. Yu, Enhanced photocatalytic activity and stability of Z-scheme Ag2CrO4-GO composite photocatalysts for organic pollutant degradation, Appl. Catal. B 164 (2015) 380-388.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ag/alpha Fe2O3-rGO novel ternary nanocomposites: synthesis, characterization, and photocatalytic activity
